subject: Databases
title: Hash Partitioning
description: A 1-minute de-risking session on Hash Partitioning.

hook.text: Hash the partition key, mod by partition count. hash(user_id) % 4 sends each user to partition 0, 1, 2, or 3.

buildup.text: Hash partitioning distributes data evenly. Even if user IDs are sequential, the hash scrambles them.

discovery.text: The downside: range queries don't work. WHERE user_id BETWEEN 100 AND 200 hits all partitions.

twist.text: Consistent hashing minimizes data movement when adding or removing nodes. Only a fraction of keys relocate.

climax.text: Cassandra and DynamoDB use consistent hashing. It's the default strategy for distributed key-value stores.

punchline.text: Hash partition for even distribution. Sacrifice range queries.

quiz.question: Why does hash partitioning break range queries?
options: Hashing scatters consecutive keys across different partitions, so ranges can't be read from one node
Hash functions only work with numeric keys
Range queries are not supported by any database
